coredata.dc:title: NiCo2O4/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for use in efficient electrochemical energy devices: An electrochemical and X-ray absorption spectroscopic investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Han-Wei
Fu, Ji-Xuan
Huang, Yu-Cheng
Lu, Ying-Rui
Kuo, Chun-Hong
Chen, Jeng-Lung
Chen, Chi-Liang
Lee, Jyh-Fu
Chen, Jin-Ming
Tsai, Yu-Chen
Ching Chou, Wu
Dong, Chung-Li
coredata.dc:description: NiCo2O4/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by the incorporation of GQDs into NiCo2O4 as electrode materials for use in electrochemical energy devices. The electronic structure and the electrochemical performance of NiCo2O4/GQDs were investigated by X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and electrochemical measurements. The rich redox active sites were revealed on the NiCo2O4/GQDs, which were formed by the strong CONi/Co interaction between NiCo2O4 and GQDs. The synergetic double-layer capacitive and pseudo-capacitive properties were suggested to provide favorable electrochemical characteristics. In/ex situ XAS elucidates local electronic and chemical environments and provides insights into the energy storage/conversion mechanisms. Analytical results demonstrate that the NiCo2O4/GQDs have strong oxygen-carbon bridges and have more Ni active sites than does NiCo2O4, favoring metal-to-ligand charge transfer at the interface between NiCo2O4 and GQDs, critically influencing electrochemical performance. This work contributes importantly to the field by elucidating an energy storage/conversion mechanism for electrochemical energy devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiCo2O4
Graphene quantum dots
X-ray absorption spectroscopy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) NCO, (b) GNCO1, and (c) GNCO2.
(a) Co K-edge and (b) Ni K-edge XAS spectra of NCO, GNCO, and standard samples. Inset in Fig. 2(a) shows pre-edge regions of Co K-edge XAS spectra.
(a) C K-edge and (b) O K-edge XAS spectra of NCO, GNCO, and standard samples. (c) EIS spectra of NCO and GNCO2.
(a) Ni and (b) Co L-edge XAS spectra of NCO, GNCO, and standard samples. Inset in center magnifies L3 regions of Ni and Co L-edge XAS spectra.
XRD spectra of GQDs, NCO, and GNCO2.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of (a) NCO and (b) GNCO2 electrodes at different scan rates; galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of (c) NCO and (d) GNCO2 electrodes at different current densities; and (e) galvanostatic charge/discharge test results of NCO and GNCO2 electrodes at different current densities, in a three-electrode system in 1 M KOH solution.

In situ (a) Co K-edge and (b) Ni K-edge XAS spectra of NCO and GNCO2 during charge/discharge process under the applied potentials in the order 0.0 V, +0.25 V, +0.5 V, +0.25 V (r0.25 V), 0.0 V (r0.0 V) in 1 M KOH solution.
Ex situ O K-edge XAS spectra of (a) NCO and (b) GNCO2 during charge/discharge process under applied potentials in the order 0.0 V, +0.25 V, +0.5 V, +0.25 V (r0.25 V), 0.0 V (r0.0 V).

Ex situ C K-edge XAS spectra of GNCO2 during charge/discharge process under applied potentials in the order 0.0 V, +0.25 V, +0.5 V, +0.25 V (r0.25 V), 0.0 V (r0.0 V). Inset shows region of features A9 and C9.
Schematic diagram of energy storage/conversion mechanisms of NCO and GNCO.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of (a) NCO//NCO and (c) GNCO2//GNCO2 symmetric supercapacitors at different scan rates; galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of (b) NCO//NCO and (d) GNCO2// GNCO2 symmetric supercapacitors at different current densities; (e) galvanostatic charge/discharge at different current densities; (f) Ragone plot (power density vs. energy density) in a two-electrode system in 1 M KOH solution.
Cyclic voltammetry curves of NCO and GNCO electrodes in 1 M KOH solution (a) without and (b) with 0.5 M methanol at scan rate of 50 mV s−1, and (c) long-term stability of NCO and GNCO samples.

head.ce:title: NiCo2O4/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for use in efficient electrochemical energy devices: An electrochemical and X-ray absorption spectroscopic investigation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, Tamkang University, Tamsui, 25137, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NiCo2O4/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by the incorporation of GQDs into NiCo2O4 as electrode materials for use in electrochemical energy devices. The electronic structure and the electrochemical performance of NiCo2O4/GQDs were investigated by X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and electrochemical measurements. The rich redox active sites were revealed on the NiCo2O4/GQDs, which were formed by the strong CONi/Co interaction between NiCo2O4 and GQDs. The synergetic double-layer capacitive and pseudo-capacitive properties were suggested to provide favorable electrochemical characteristics. In/ex situ XAS elucidates local electronic and chemical environments and provides insights into the energy storage/conversion mechanisms. Analytical results demonstrate that the NiCo2O4/GQDs have strong oxygen-carbon bridges and have more Ni active sites than does NiCo2O4, favoring metal-to-ligand charge transfer at the interface between NiCo2O4 and GQDs, critically influencing electrochemical performance. This work contributes importantly to the field by elucidating an energy storage/conversion mechanism for electrochemical energy devices.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last several decades, rapid economic and industrial growth in developed and developing countries around the world have led to huge global energy consumption, which have increased climate change and environmental degradation. The development of renewable energy technologies is essential to tackling environmental issues and slowing down the depletion of natural resources. Electrochemical energy devices, such as supercapacitors and direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs), are commonly integrated into renewable energy systems because of their excellent storage/conversion stability. Owing to their high power density, excellent reversibility, high charge/discharge rate, and long-term stability, supercapacitors are regarded as important energy storage devices. Supercapacitors can be divided by their energy storage mechanism into those that exhibit electrochemical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and those that exhibit pseudo-capacitance. The electrochemical performance of supercapacitors is currently being improved by the development of hybrid electrode materials, which have attracted considerable attention because they combine both EDLC and pseudo-capacitance. Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) directly use renewable methanol as a fuel, and have also attracted substantial attention. The advantages of DMFCs in regenerative power supply are high energy conversion efficiency, low operating temperature, ease of handling/processing and low environmental pollution, which are all critical features of renewable energy systems.

Numerous studies have reported that the ternary nickel cobaltite (NiCo2O4) spinel structure is a promising material for use in supercapacitors and DMFCs owing to its variable oxidation state and favorable electrochemical activity. Spinel structures of ternary oxides of the form AB2O4 can be divided into normal and inverse spinel structures, depending on the distribution of divalent and trivalent cations among the octahedral and tetrahedral sites [1]. These ternary oxides are expected to undergo a range of redox reactions owing to the synergistic effects of both nickel and cobalt ion. For instance, Gao et al. synthesized NiCo2O4 nanosheets with high pseudo-capacitive performance. The high performance was attributed to the unique structural features of the NiCo2O4 nanosheets and their exceptionally high surface area, which makes their surfaces highly electroactive, facilitating charge transport [2]. Chen et al. efficiently synthesized a porous NiCo2O4 nanostructure that supports fast ion transport along short ion migration pathways on account of its highly ordered porous structure. This nanostructure has potential for use in supercapacitors [3]. Qian et al. reported that NiCo2O4 redox reactions of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ are crucial for methanol oxidation, and are believed to contribute to the high electrocatalytic ability of DMFCs [4]. However, the poor electronic conductivity of NiCo2O4 greatly limits its further development for use in renewable energy systems. To overcome the inherent limitations of NiCo2O4, the incorporation of carbon materials has been shown to be an effective strategy. Adán-Más et al. claimed that the electrochemical performance of nickel-cobalt oxides was enhanced by combining them with electrochemically reduced graphene oxides, and found that phase transformation and microstructural cracking are possible causes of its capacity fading. [5] Zhang et al. prepared NiCo2O4 on 3D graphene foam with high capacitive performance, which they attributed to the synergistic effects of high electrical conductivity and the large surface area of the 3D graphene foam, as well as to the catalytic activity of NiCo2O4 [6]. Gao et al. developed flexible high-performance NiCo2O4 hybrid structures (nanosheets on nanoarrays) on carbon cloth. They fabricated an asymmetric supercapacitor device in the form of NiCo2O4/carbon cloth//porous graphene paper with high capacitive retention over 5000 mechanical bendings. [7]. Kong et al. also constructed an asymmetric supercapacitor from NiCo2O4 nanowire arrays with polypyrrole, which retained its cycling stability after 5000 instances of charging-discharging. [8]. Wei et al. found that NiCo2O4-coated graphene had impressive specific capacitance when it was used as a robust and conductive substrate for the growth of NiCo2O4. The high conductivity of graphene was associated with highly conductive paths for electron transport which also supported ultrathin NiCo2O4 sheets with a large surface area, providing many active redox sites, and thereby improving electrochemical performance [9]. Das et al. developed NiCo2O4/graphene with greater electrocatalytic activity than bare NiCo2O4 and provided insights into the synergistic effects of the graphene and NiCo2O4, which greatly improve greatly the performance of DMFCs [10].

GQDs have a single atomic layer of nano-sized graphite oxygen functionalities at their edges, providing unusual properties. GQDs have a wide variety of oxygen functional groups and edge-enriched planes, making them attractive for use in supercapacitors and DMFCs. Hassan et al. reported that the edge effects of GQDs improve the accessibility of electrolyte ions [11]. Hu et al. also claimed that the use of conductive GQDs in the fabrication of supercapacitors enhanced their capacitance performance as a result of the synergy between the formation of a continuous film-like structure and increased specific surface area [12]. As noted above, synergistic combinations of NiCo2O4 and GQDs can be exploited as promising electrode materials with improved electrochemical performance of devices in which they are used. Energy storage/conversion mechanisms are involved in changes in the local electronic and chemical environments of the activating materials. Element-specific X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) involves the transition of electrons from their core levels to unoccupied electronic states; it provides crucial information about the local electronic and chemical environments of constituent elements in a hybrid material. In/ex situ XAS is effective for monitoring changes in the atomic and electronic structures of energy storage/conversion materials under working conditions [13]. This work provides a deep insight into the electrochemical properties and energy storage/conversion mechanism of NiCo2O4/GQDs by taking a microcosmic perspective of their electronic and atomic structures. XAS is used to elucidate how the electronic/atomic structures of NCO are modified by decorating GQDs and in/ex situ XAS is used to identify active sites and determine the variation of electronic/atomic structures under working conditions. The origin of the enhanced capacitive properties is thus identified. In/ex situ XAS is used to address the limitations of previous studies. A symmetric two-electrode supercapacitor device is constructed and its capacitive performance is evaluated.

Nickel sulfate hexahydrate (NiSO4·6H2O) and citric acid (CA) were obtained from J.T. Baker. Cobalt sulfate heptahydrate (CoSO4·7H2O) was obtained from Vetec. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. All chemical solutions were prepared using a Milli-Q water purification system (Milli-Q, USA).

GQDs were synthesized from citric acid (CA) using the bottom-up method [14]. Different amounts (2.5 and 15 g) of citric acid were heated at 90 °C and the GQDs were synthesized as follows. Citric acid was pyrolyzed until it had become orange. Then, 50 mL DI water was added to the orange liquid to yield solutions with different concentrations of GQDs. The resulting samples were called GQD1 and GQD2, respectively. NiCo2O4 (NCO) and NiCo2O4/GQDs (GNCO) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1.05 g of NiSO4·6H2O, 2.25 g of CoSO4·7H2O and 3.60 g of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of a solution of GQDs (GQD1 and GQD2) in DI, with continuous stirring for 30 min until the solute was completely dissolved. Each mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 24 h. After heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the obtained precipitate was washed in DI water several times and then collected by centrifugation. After drying at 80 °C, the precursors were annealed at 400 °C for three hours to obtain the resulting samples, which were named NCO, GNCO1, and GNCO2. The obtained samples were washed with deionized water to remove the remaining reagents and collected for subsequent characterization.

The morphologies of the NCO, GNCO1 and GNCO2 were determined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-7410 F, JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a D8 Discover (Bruker) X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Electrochemical measurements were made using either a two- or a three-electrode system and an electrochemical analyzer (Autolab, model PGSTAT30, Eco Chemie, Netherlands). The supercapacitors with conventional three-electrode systems comprised as-prepared sample-modified graphite electrodes, a platinum wire counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) reference electrode in 1 M KOH. The symmetric supercapacitors with conventional two-electrode systems consisted of two as-prepared sample-modified electrodes in 1 M KOH. In DMFCs applications, the as-prepared sample-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 0.07 cm2) was used as a working electrode. All working electrodes were fabricated by mixing the electroactive materials with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). The mass loading of the as-prepared samples that were coated on the electrodes was determined using a microbalance. Synchrotron XAS spectra were obtained at beamlines BL20A and BL17C of the National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC), Taiwan. In situ XAS was performed using a homemade in situ electrochemical cell.

The surface morphologies of the NCO and GNCO samples (GNCO1 and GNCO2) were examined by FESEM. Fig. 1 (a) shows that NiCo2O4 was well-distributed with a size range of 10–20 nm. The introduction of GQDs into NCO yields a product GNCO1, which has smaller particles and distinct particle/particle interfaces (Fig. 1(b)). The surface morphology of GNCO2 is more prominent than that of NCO or GNCO1. Larger agglomerated particles are found, suggesting that the introduction of GQDs makes NiCo2O4 more amorphous, increasing the amount of contained binding and conducting agents, favoring the formation of agglomerates in the GNCO2 (Fig. 1(c)) [15]. These findings suggest that the amount of GQDs may influence the growth of NCO and GNCO samples and further affect the electrochemical performance of the samples.

To gain insight into the electronic and atomic structures of NCO and GNCO samples, Fig. 2 presents the Co and Ni K-edge XAS spectra of the NCO, GNCO, and standard samples. The Co and Ni K-edge XAS spectra reveal that NCO and GNCO samples comprise mostly Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ [16]. The Co K-edge XAS spectra in Fig. 2(a) include a weak pre-edge absorption peak and a strong main absorption peak. The pre-edge absorption peak is attributed to the electric dipole-forbidden transition of the 1 s electron to the 3d unoccupied orbital, and the changes in the position and intensity of that pre-edge absorption peak provide important information about the oxidation state and local structural symmetry. Pre-edge features have been extensively used to characterize the spinel structure. Marked increases in the intensity of pre-edge features as a result of non-centrosymmetric atomic arrangement are commonly observed because local distortions cause 3d-4p orbital mixing and metal-ligand overlap [17–19]. The weak intensity of the pre-edge feature in the NCO and GNCO samples indicates mixing of the octahedral-tetrahedral environment.

The main absorption edges of the Co K-edge of NCO and GNCO samples resemble that of reference Co3O4, suggesting NCO and GNCO samples contain a mixture of Co2+/Co3+ in an octahedral-tetrahedral environment [18]. The inset in Fig. 2(a) shows the pre-edge regions of the Co K-edge XAS spectra following subtraction of the background. The intensities of the pre-peak regions of the spectra of the GNCO samples clearly decline as the amount of introduced GQDs increases, supporting the claim that the introduction of GQDs into GNCO2 causes it to adopt a more centrosymmetric arrangement. The Ni K-edge XAS spectra of NCO and GNCOs in Fig. 2(b) include a strong main absorption peak and a rather weak pre-edge feature, indicating that their structures are primarily centrosymmetric [20]. The main absorption peak is shifted to higher energies in the GNCO samples, revealing a possibly higher oxidation state, Ni3+, at the octahedral sites in the NiCo2O4 spinel structure. The Co and Ni K-edge XAS spectra demonstrate that the NCO and GNCO samples have the spinel structure in which Co ions are at the tetrahedral and octahedral sites and Ni ions are at the octahedral sites [21,22]. These results also reveal the presence of Co and Ni with higher oxidation states at the octahedral sites in the GNCO samples. In previous studies, spinel structures with most of the Co3+ and Ni3+ at octahedral sites have been shown to exhibit greatly enhanced electrochemical activity [23,24]. Thus, the modification of GNCO samples with GQDs is expected to favor their use as electrode materials in supercapacitors and DMFCs.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) display the C K-edge and O K-edge of GQDs, NCO and GNCO samples. The C K-edge XAS spectra in Fig. 3(a) are associated with electron transitions from the C 1s core level to C 2p unoccupied states. The C K-edge XAS spectra are characterized by the marked features (peaks A3, B3, and C3). Features A3 and C3 at about 285 and 292 eV are associated with transitions from C 1s to unoccupied π* and σ* states, respectively. They can be strongly affected by chemically functionalized carbon atoms and/or defects in the carbon-based materials. Feature B3 (including sub-peaks B3’, B3’’, and B3’’’) is attributable to interlayer states or transitions from the oxygen-containing functional groups that correspond to CO π* in the hydroxyl groups (B3′), O-CO π* in the epoxide groups (B3″), and CO π* in the carboxyl and carbonyl groups (B3’’’) [25–27]. Feature A3 of GNCO was shifted to a slightly lower photon energy than that of GQDs and the intensity of feature A3 increased with the amount of GQDs introduced, reflecting the fact that the density of the unoccupied states with π* character in GNCO exceeds that of GQDs [28]. This result suggests the formation of strong CONi/Co bonds between the GQDs and NiCo2O4. The GQDs act as electron donors and charge is transferred from GQDs to NiCo2O4. Additionally, the analysis of the electronic structure revealed CC π* and CC σ* features in both GNCO samples, demonstrating that the GNCO samples with GQD modification maintained their graphitic sp2 structure, favoring the electrical conductivity. To confirm the favorable electrical conductivity, Fig. 3(c) compares the EIS spectra of NCO and GNCO. The EIS was collected at frequencies from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with an AC perturbation of 5 mV at 0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The equivalent series resistance (Rs) is obtained from the intercept of the real axis, which represents the interface resistance between the electrolyte and the electrode materials. The slope of the EIS curve yields the diffusive resistance against the diffusion/transport of electrolyte ions throughout the electrode materials. A straighter line indicates easier ion diffusion and more favorable capacitive behavior in the structure of the electrode materials. The Rs value of GNCO2 is lower than that of NCO and the line of GNCO2 is straighter and has a greater slope, revealing that GNCO2 has the better capacitive behavior [29–31]. The observed variation in the relative intensity of features B3 of GQDs and GNCO corresponds to variations in the corresponding carbon atom environments as a result of changes in the proportion of oxygen-containing groups in the synthesis process, revealing a spectral correlation between the GQDs and NiCo2O4. With respect to the GQDs and GNCO samples, the intensity of feature B3 is reduced upon the introduction of GQDs into NCO, and this reduction is accompanied by an increase in the intensity of the CC π* and CC σ* features. This analytical result indicates that the modification of GNCO with GQDs in a synthetic process at a high annealing temperature (400 °C) removed some of the oxygen-containing groups from the surfaces of the GQDs. The significant π* and σ* features in GNCO samples suggest crystalline graphitic structures of GQDs, and the partial restoration of their graphitic structure and conductivity. The edge plane of graphitic carbon has more electrochemically active sites for electron transfer and adsorption than does the basal plane, GNCO2, and it has more edge terminal oxygen-containing groups, which provide greater opportunities to optimize the electrochemical and capacitive performance [32,33], as revealed by the Fig. 3(a), in which feature B3 is higher in GNCO2 than in GNCO1.

Fig. 3(b) presents the O K-edge XAS spectra of GQDs, NCO, GNCO samples, and standard samples. The O K-edge XAS spectra of GQDs have three main features. Features D3 and E3 at ∼532 and ∼535 eV are associated with π* character; depend strongly on the chemical environment of the oxygen bonds, and are attributable to π* transitions of the CO in carboxyl and carbonyl groups, and O-C–O π* transitions from epoxide groups. The broad feature (F3) at ∼540 eV is attributed to the σ* transitions of a mixture of OH, CO, or CO [27,34]. Features G3, H3, and I3, which are attributed to the NiCo2O4 species are observed in the O K-edge XAS spectra of NCO. These three features in the O K-edge XAS spectra of the GNCO samples depend on electron transition to O 2p orbitals that are hybridized with the Ni/Co 3d orbitals and Ni/Co 4sp orbitals. The XAS spectra of GNCO samples are superpositions of the spectra of the references (GQDs, NiO, CoO and Co3O4) perhaps because the samples comprise multiple species (carbon and bimetallic materials). Based on the standard samples, features G3 and H3 are assigned to the intermediate oxides, cobalt oxide and nickel oxide, respectively. The intensities of pre-edge peaks G3 and H3 are proportional to the degree of mixing of empty O 2p character and transition metal 3d-O 2p character. The increase in the pre-edge peak intensity upon modification with GQDs demonstrates that an increase in empty O 2p character markedly favors the formation of strong covalent bonds [35]. Notably, the change in the pre-peak region of the O K-edge XAS spectra of the GNCO samples with an increase in the amount of GQDs is the opposite to that of feature A3 in the C K-edge. A strong CONi/Co interaction and the metal-to-ligand charge transfer are suggested to be significant at the interface between NiCo2O4 and GQDs.

Fig. 4 (a) and (b) present Ni and Co L-edge XAS spectra of the NCO and GNCO samples. The peak position and the spectral line shape of the 3d metal L-edge strongly depend on the local atomic and electronic structures of the metal ions. The central insets in Fig. 4 magnify the L3 regions of the Ni and Co L-edge. The L3 regions of the Ni L- and Co L-edge reveal that the NCO and GNCO samples contained mainly mixed oxidation states of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ [36]. The above results (Figs. 3 and 4) support the following possible growth mechanism of NCO and GNCO upon the introduction of GQDs. First, the pre-edge feature (∼532 eV) in the O K-edge XAS spectra arises from the complexity of the multi-electron correlation effects in Ni/Co 3d-O 2p hybridization. The intensity of this pre-edge feature in the O K-edge XAS spectra increases with the introduction of GQDs into NiCo2O4, indicating that the amount of unoccupied Ni/Co 3d-O 2p hybridized states increases. These changes are accompanied by a decline in Ni and Co L-edge XAS intensity (Fig. 4) and provide evidence of an increase in electron density at O sites and a drop in electron density at Ni/Co sites, demonstrating the strongly p-d-hybridized states of the GNCO samples. Second, the intensities of feature A3 in the C K-edge XAS spectra of the GNCO samples increase with the amount of GQDs introduced, whereas the intensity of the pre-edge feature in the O K-edge XAS spectra declined, showing that charge is transferred from GQDs to NiCo2O4. The peak B4 of the C K-edge XAS spectra of the GNCO samples increased with the amount of introduced GQDs. These changes are correlated with the interaction between NiCo2O4 and GQDs, suggesting that strong coordinated interactions among neighboring atoms favor interfacial charge transport in a GNCO-reinforced structure, increasing the efficiency of storage/conversion in electrochemical energy devices in which it is a used.

Fig. 5 compares the XRD patterns of NCO and GNCO2. The crystalline structures of NCO and GNCO2 were indexed as the inverse spinel structure of NiCo2O4 (JCPDS card no. 73-1702) [21,37]. The sample that had been modified with GQDs had a broad peak at about 26.4°, corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphitic structure, indicating that smaller GQDs were stacked in a disordered manner [38]. Following modification with GQDs, GNCO2 yields the very weak and broad diffraction peak of a graphitic structure (denoted as *) as well as diffraction peaks of NiCo2O4. This result suggests the successful introduction of GQDs into NCO. However, the slight reduction of the intensity of the diffraction peak of GNCO2 implies its poor crystallinity, which provides more favorable paths than a structure with high crystallinity for the penetration and transportation of ions, further favoring capacitive performance [39,40].

The supercapacitive performance of NCO and GNCO samples was evaluated by CV and galvanostatic charges/discharge in a three-electrode system. Fig. 6 (a) and (b) compare the CV curves of NCO and GNCO2 at different scan rates with a voltage window of 0 to 0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl in 1 M KOH solution. For both NCO and GNCO2, the current clearly increased with the scan rate, indicating favorable capacitive behavior. Two pairs of redox peaks are observed for the NCO, indicating that its capacitive characteristics are mostly pseudo-capacitive. The two pairs of redox current peaks are attributable to the reversible Faradaic redox reactions of Ni2+/Ni3+ (at 0.2∼0.3 V) and Co2+/Co3+ (at 0.3∼0.4 V). However, the CV curve of GNCO2 was quasi-rectangular with two pairs of broad redox peaks. The quasi-rectangular shape reflects fast charge–discharge processes, which are characteristic of EDLC, which arises from the introduction of GQDs. The area under the CV curve of GNCO2 was larger than that under the CV curve of NCO, revealing that GNCO2 had greater capacitance than NCO.

Fig. 6(c) and (d) compare the galvanostatic charges/discharges of NCO and GNCO2 at various current densities. The charge curves of GNCO2 are approximately triangular with a slight curvature. This result is attributed to the combination of EDLC and pseudo-capacitance as a result of the presence of both NiCo2O4 and GQDs. The resulting specific capacitance is calculated using the following formula. C = (I Δt)/(ΔV m), where C denotes specific capacitance (F g−1); I is the charge/discharge current (A); Δt is the discharge time (s); ΔV is the voltage change during discharge (V), and m is the mass of the active materials in the electrode (g). At a current density of 1 A g−1, the specific capacitance of the GNCO2 was calculated to be 302 F g−1, which exceeds that of NCO (112 F g−1). GNCO2 exhibited much higher specific capacitance than NCO at the same current density, mainly owing to its interconnected structure of NiCo2O4 and GQDs, forming highly conductive channels as effective electron/ion paths and faster transport and improves the conductivity of GNCO2. The galvanostatic charge/discharge test results for NCO and GNCO2 at various current densities indicate that the specific capacitances of NCO and GNCO2 gradually decrease as the current density increases. As plotted in Fig. 6(e), GNCO2 exhibited a good rate capability with 93% capacitance retention, which is much higher than that (88%) of NCO, indicating that GNCO2 has great potential for use in the development of electrochemical energy devices.

To evaluate the performance of supercapacitor devices that are built with NCO and GNCO2 electrodes, symmetric two-electrode supercapacitors were assembled using two similar NCO or GNCO2 electrodes. Fig. 11 (a) and (c) plot CV curves and galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of NCO//NCO and GNCO2//GNCO2 symmetric supercapacitors with a voltage window of 0 to 1 V in 1 M KOH solution. The CV curves of the NCO//NCO and GNCO2//GNCO2 supercapacitors exhibit no obvious distortion as the scan rate is increased, revealing the high reversibility of the system and the rapid diffusion of electrolyte ions toward the electrode/electrolyte interface. The CV curves of NCO//NCO include two pairs of poorly defined redox peaks, which may correspond to the reversible Faradaic redox reactions of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+, indicating that the capacitance of NCO//NCO mostly originates from the redox pseudo-capacitance of NiCo2O4. However, the nearly rectangular shape of the CV curves of GNCO2//GNCO2 and their poorly defined redox peaks shows that its capacitance comprises EDLC and pseudo-capacitance. The galvanostatic charge/discharge curves in Fig. 11(b) and (d) have slightly distorted triangular shapes and exhibit) only a modest IR drop, verifying the perfect capacitive behavior of each device. Increasing the current density slightly reduced the specific capacitance, indicating that both symmetric supercapacitors had a good rate capability (Fig. 11(e)). The specific capacitance of GNCO2//GNCO2 is calculated to be 35 F g −1 at the low current density of 0.25 A g−1, corresponding to a specific capacitance of a single electrode of 140 F g−1 (obtained by multiplying the specific capacitance of GNCO2//GNCO2 by four), which greatly exceeds that of NCO//NCO. Clearly, GNCO2, which consists of NiCo2O4 and GQDs, exhibits consistently good capacitive performance. Fig. 11(f) presents the Ragone plots of the NCO//NCO and GNCO2//GNCO2 symmetric supercapacitors, calculated from the galvanostatic charge/discharge results. The energy density (E) and power density (P) were calculated using the formulas E = 0.5 C ΔV2 and P = E Δt−1. As a result, the GNCO2//GNCO2 symmetric supercapacitors exhibited an energy density of 5 Wh kg-1 at a power density of 125 W kg−1 and 3.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1000 W kg−1; these results are comparable with those reported elsewhere [43–46].

Fig. 12 compares CV and chronoamperometric (CA) results for NCO and GNCO samples for use in DMFCs. Fig. 12(a) and (b) plot CV curves of NCO and GNCO electrodes in 1 M KOH solution without and with 0.5 M methanol from 0 to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The electrocatalytic mechanism of methanol oxidation on the NiCo2O4-based materials is given by the following equations [47,48].
(1) NiOOH/CoOOH+OH− ↔ Ni(OH)2/ Co(OH)2 + H2O+e−
(2) NiOOH+CH3OH + 1.25O2 ↔ Ni(OH)2 + CO2 + 1.5H2O
(3) CoOOH+CH3OH + 1.25O2 ↔ Co(OH)2 + CO2 + 1.5H2O

As indicated in Fig. 12(a), NiCo2O4-based materials in an alkaline electrolyte yield well-defined redox peaks owing to reversible Faradaic redox reactions of Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+, described by Eq. (1). In contrast, GNCO2 exhibited a larger integrated CV area with broad redox peaks, revealing that its electrochemical properties are enhanced by the presence of more efficient active sites, which favor the rapid transmission of electrons and ions at the electrolyte/electrode interface. The electrocatalytic activity of methanol oxidation in 1 M KOH with 0.5 M methanol was tested under the same conditions. After methanol was added in the alkaline solution, the direct catalytic oxidation reaction of methanol sped up Co(OH)2/CoOOH and Ni(OH)2/NiOOH transformation at the active sites, consistent with Eqs. (2) and (3), increasing the anodic peak current. Similarly, GNCO2 exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic activity and favorable kinetics in methanol electrooxidation as a result of the synergy between NiCo2O4 and GQDs. Long-term stability tests were performed using a CA in 1 M KOH with 0.5 M methanol after 1000s at 0.6 V. GNCO2 exhibited excellent long-term stability in methanol electrooxidation. The strong CONi/Co interaction at the interface between NiCo2O4 and GQDs favors methanol electrooxidation and the long-term maintenance of reactivity. The above results clearly reveal that the as-obtained material NiCo2O4/GQDs has potential for commercial use as electrochemical energy device.

In this work, the excellent electrochemical activity of GNCO samples was demonstrated using in/ex situ XAS and electrochemical measurements. GNCO samples exhibit a significant interfacial interaction between NiCo2O4 and GQDs, and presents the abundance of redox active sites, which favor metal-to-ligand charge transfer at the interface. A strong CONi/Co interaction is suggested for the the conductive path of electron/ion, improving electrochemical performance. In combination, double-layer capacitance and pseudo-capacitance are responsible for the favorable electrochemical and capacitive characteristics of NiCo2O4 that is decorated with GQDs. In/ex situ XAS are used to identify that Ni is more active than Co sites, and to elucidate the origin of the enhanced electrochemical performance. NCO//NCO and GNCO2//GNCO2 symmetric supercapacitor devices are constructed and provide a good basis for the development of electrochemical energy devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the contracts MoST 108-2118-E-032-003-MY3, MoST 107-2112-M-032-004-MY3 from Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan and AS-iMATE-108-23 from Academia Sinica.
